url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2q3iCnDgDXk
title: Will Burgeoning Core Inflation Pressures from AI Capex Force the Fed to Crash the Stock Market?

We want to get the macro picture.
Joining us right now is Darius Dale, the
founder and CEO of the research firm 42
Macro. Darius, great to see you here. I
I wonder if you could maybe put it this
into perspective here because there are
a lot of conflicting opinions about
where we stand right now. You have a
an economy overall that appears to be
holding up, an equity market that
despite the downdraft today still not
far from its record highs, but signals
coming out of the bond market that are
causing a lot of concern out there. How
do you read it?
Uh thanks again for having me, Romaine.
It's always a pleasure. So, things are
fine from an economic perspective and
you can make the case that they're
actually too fine. We have nominal GDP
tracking up about 10% on a 3-month
annualized basis and so for this and for
a variety of other reasons, we think the
Fed is falling behind the curve. When
you look at that the market's pricing in
for neutral and we take in a cutting
cycle, the minimum value on the IS curve
out 5 years or in a hiking cycle, we
take the maximum value on the IS curve
out 5 years, you can see that the
minimum value now the market's estimate
of neutral if we were still on a cutting
cycle, which we may not be,
is now higher than the effective funds
rate. And then the effective funds rate
is actually two rate hikes below, sorry,
two two rate hikes below from where it
actually needs to be just for the Fed to
get back to neutral. So, this is a Fed
that's falling behind the curve and
should actually contribute to bond
market volatility in the months ahead as
it struggles to catch up. Well, that's
what I'm curious about here. I mean, the
whole narrative on Kevin Warsh, who's
expected to be sworn in as Fed chair
before the week is out, the whole
narrative on him was that he was going
to be a little bit more dovish when it
came to monetary policy. I know a lot
has changed since his name was first
floated as a potential Fed chair, but do
you get the sense here that he will try
to stick by, I guess that longer-term
structural narrative of easier monetary
policy or give in to I guess what the
economic data might actually be telling
him?
I think he's going to wind up giving in
to what the data are telling him and the
reason is twofold. One, you're actually
getting you know, ever so slight signals
from the administration that they're
okay with the Federal Reserve actually
maintaining some some of the inflation
fighting credibility here. You got
Bostic signaling that hey, the Fed can
wait on cutting interest rates.
President Trump was out with similar
commentary this past weekend. And then
secondarily, if you look at the metrics
that Kevin Warsh himself was
highlighting, specifically trim mean
CPI, we're now tracking up at 3.4%
through month annualized. That number's
higher than the six-month annualized
rate of 3.2%, higher than the
year-over-year rate of change of 2.9%.
We got a strong positive impulse in the
inflation statistic that Kevin Warsh was
going to anchor on to lower interest
rates. And so, in our opinion, he's
going to wind up at an FOMC where most
of his colleagues there are going to be
moving in a hawkish direction, and they
should be because the bond market is
telling them to.
I do want to get your thoughts on
something that one of our previous
guest, Julie Biel, brought up. And it
was the idea of what artificial
intelligence might actually mean for the
economy, and particularly what the
response might have to be by the
government. The idea that whatever
upsides we're seeing in the market from
this with regards to the cap ex bends,
it might come, at least in the interim,
with some sort of cost
to jobs, to American jobs. And the idea
as to whether the Fed, or really even
fiscal policy makers, have a response,
or even can have a response to that
should it actually materialize.
Well, they can and they should. I think
that's a that's a that's where you and I
can grab some beers for this kind of
conversation. But uh the reality is they
can and they should. That's why we have
a democracy. That's why we elect uh
public officials to answer and and solve
these big problems. Uh but of course, uh
but one thing I would highlight just
from the set of investors is the fact
that AI, at least in 2026, is a roughly
$800 billion
positive demand shock for resources and
positive demand shock for capital that's
expected to grow to about $1.2 trillion
next year. So, as disinflationary and
and as productivity enhancing as the
technology may be longer term, in the
interim, it is actually pushing up
demand in the economy and pushing up
inflation. And we can see this across a
variety of inflation statistics. Every
single core inflation measure, where you
look at PPI, CPI, or PCE, is in a strong
positive impulse or a weak positive
impulse at a level that is significantly
higher than the Fed funds 2% target. So,
this So, kind of tie this up though,
because we get to this idea of whether
we even need a Fed to do anything at
this moment. I mean, you know, maybe
they need to hike to fight inflation,
maybe they don't. But, there's also been
a lot of talk of why is there any sort
of haste, if you will, to do anything
right now, given how well, let's face
it, that economy has held up, at least
based on the official economic data, and
given how well markets have held up,
given, you know, the recent record highs
that were, well, just, I don't know, a
few days ago.
Well, that's a great question, Romaine.
That's exactly what I said with your
colleagues on surveillance a few weeks
ago, which is if the Fed does anything
this year, they're going to cause
problems in asset markets, because we
clearly have evidence from the bond
market itself and from reported
inflation data that the Fed is falling
behind the curve. So, if they ease
monetary policy, you're going to
exacerbate the meltdown of the bond
market. However, if they tight monetary
policy in response to the
[clears throat] build-up of inflation
pressures, and they're obviously going
to cause an unwind of the crowded bull's
positioning, and particularly where
everyone is crowded concentrated right
now, which is in AI. Fun fact on AI, 48%
of the S&P 500 is now tech and
communication services, which is higher
than it was at the peak of the dot-com
bubble. Is that a risk though right now,
because I mean, there is someone make
the argument that this complex the
complexion of this rally is a lot
different. The AI bubble was a lot of
companies that with no profitability,
questionable revenue growth, and this AI
boom is being driven by, let's face it,
the biggest companies in the world with
some of the biggest cash stockpiles in
the world, who can borrow at relatively
low rates.
Well, they still have to meet investor
expectations for their sales, their
earnings, their cash flows, their return
on invested capital, their return on
equity. Those expectations are going to
go They're growing exponentially
alongside the CapEx. And so, ultimately,
investors are going to want their money
back and this stuff tells me actually
back to your previous question, which is
what are the societal ramifications of
this? You know, we've been very much on
record for the past few years that AI is
going to cause a, you know, kind of low
higher low fire dynamic in the labor
market to continue and in our opinion we
think that's going to accelerate in the
coming years in terms of its depressing
impact on labor demand. And if that's
true, then you start to think about
what's going to happen from the
perspective of the political response.
You're going to be talking about
nationwide wealth taxes and
redistribution. You're going to be
talking about a heavy hand of regulation
coming from the other side of the
political aisle in response to some of
these dynamics and so at the end of the
day no matter how profitable these
companies are and how you know, game
changing this technology is, there is
another side of the trade that investors
won't be prepared for in terms of how
much concentration we're seeing at the
current juncture. All right, Darius.
Great stuff. Darius Dale, founder, CEO
over at the research firm 42 Macro.
